title: Multiple containers cannot publish a port from different IPs
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

Multiple containers cannot publish the same port from different IPs.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Run a container with port forwarding: 
`podman run -it -p 127.0.0.1:9090:9090 alpine`

2. Run a second container forwarding the same port but from a different IP
`podman run -it -p 127.0.0.42:9090:9090 alpine`

**Describe the results you received:**

The second container errors with

```
Error: error preparing container 4a8aa67b1ef21574cf3e4b3bcade2183349d081e28b77e3cc2a35aea1dd8c47c for attach: cannot listen on the TCP port: listen tcp4 :9090: bind: address already in use
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

The second container should run.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

This is due to the removal of the host portion by Podman in the machine ([link](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/libpod/networking_linux.go#L65)).

I can think of two potential solutions:

1) Create loopback aliases for the IP inside the Podman machine

    This is easily done with the command `sudo ifconfig lo:N <ip> up`, where N is a unique number, so I imagine implementing this in Podman should be straightforward. There is a drawback however, since the IP no longer resolves to the host, containers won't be able to access host services listening on that IP. For example, if I have a service on the host listening on `192.168.64.2:80` and run a container that publishes `192.168.64.2:9090`, the host service will not be reachable by any Podman container.

2) Assign a random port inside the Podman machine

    The container inside the Podman machine publishes to a random open port on `*` and the gvproxy forwards to it. For example say a user runs a container that publishes `192.168.64.2:9090`, internally Podman will instead publish `*:41356` in the machine and on the host gvproxy will be told to forward `192.168.64.2:9090` to `*:41356`. I don't know enough to evaluate the complexity in implementing this solution, however.

I haven't delved much into qemu and gvproxy, so there might be solutions there that I don't know of.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.1.1
API Version:  4.1.1

```

comments.body:
I don't think the first solution will work. The gvproxy process forwards the port to the eth0 interface so the packages will not reach the other interfaces.

The second solution sounds complicated and hard to maintain (where would we store what ports are used?).

I don't really have a good handle on this one but open to ideas. Does it work with docker-desktop?

Ah you're right, the first solution doesn't work, sorry should have tried it out first. With `gvproxy` configured to forward `127.0.0.1:9090` to `127.0.0.1:9090` traffic did not reach a service (eg: I used netcat) listening on that address and port, and not even if listening on `*:9090` instead.

The second solution might not be too complicated if we don't need to store/manage the random port mappings. If all we need to do is generate the random port and feed it to gvproxy on one side and update [`convertPortMappings`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/libpod/networking_linux.go#L65) for the other side then might be straightforward enough.

A port published on multiple IPs is not an issue with Docker Desktop.

> The second solution might not be too complicated if we don't need to store/manage the random port mappings. If all we need to do is generate the random port and feed it to gvproxy on one side and update [`convertPortMappings`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/libpod/networking_linux.go#L65) for the other side then might be straightforward enough.

But we have to store them, otherwise we cannot remove them when using iptables to forward the port (i.e. when running as root in the VM).

> A port published on multiple IPs is not an issue with Docker Desktop.

Any idea how it is handled there?

I wasn't aware that iptables is used in the machine for port forwarding, could you point me to the code that handles this? Having said that, I ran `podman -c podman-machine-default-root run -it -p 9090:9090 alpine` and with `iptables -L` I don't see any port forwarding rules, although my knowledge of iptables is fairly limited so I might be missing something.

Took a look at how Docker Desktop might handle this. When running `docker -it -p 127.0.0.42:9090:9090 alpine` I see that the `com.docker.backend` process is binding to `127.0.0.42:9090` on my macbook and inside the Docker VM I see `dockerd` is also listening on `127.0.0.42:9090`. The interesting part is that `ifconfig` does not reveal any Docker VM interfaces with the `127.0.0.42` IP address.

You can bind to any 127.x.x.x address on linux without problems.

You have to use `iptables -L -t nat` inside the VM to see the port forwarding rules. 

I guess docker can do because there port forwarder works differently, somehow it can send the packages to localhost?
Maybe we can fix the at the gvproxy level so it can also handle this use case but I am not familiar with gvproxy so I have no idea if that would be possible.

Oh thanks for the more precise iptables command, I do see it now.

Tried something a little different with Docker Desktop and learned a thing. Ran `docker run -it -p 192.168.64.1:9090:9090 alpine` and I see in the Docker VM that it's listening to `127.0.0.1:55000` and its iptables lists a rule forwarding that IP+port to `172.17.0.3:9090`. When I then run another container with `-p 192.168.64.2:9090:9090` I see `127.0.0.1:55001` being bound to in the VM with a similar iptables rule. So it does seem to use a port inside the VM different from the container's published port.
I've also just found this [article](https://www.docker.com/blog/how-docker-desktop-networking-works-under-the-hood/) that could be helpful, but most of it is over my head at the moment.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Not AFAIK. I believe this requires gvproxy changes.

I don't think you can change gvproxy to fix this. Podman needs a extra db to keep track of the port mapping since they can not be one to one for such use case. If it knows the port mapping it can call gvproxy like it does at the moment.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@Luap99 @mheon any chance this can be worked on any time soon.
nope, r/n we are absolutely buried .. lets discuss next week
